blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following rule:

blocks.description: This equation defines the total differential in terms of partial derivatives.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of a function of one change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$ and $y$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: The figure illustrates a point moving on the surface defined by $z=f(x,y)$ as $x$ and $y$ change.

blocks.latex: \text{total change in } z = \text{change in } z \text{ due to the change in } x
blocks.description: This emphasizes how $z$ changes with respect to $x$.

blocks.text: Let $P$ be a point $(x, y, z)$ on a surface, and let $P$ move to a new position $Q$ near to $P$. The change of position is noted by vector notation, i.e., $\Delta x$ is change on the surface (y being kept constant).
